sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01
sources.specific_claim: c1b7 definition and hierarchy — herbal/multi-species ley as UKHab level 4 cropland code

ecology.description: Herbal or multi-species ley (c1b7) is a sown grassland containing a minimum of three functional groups — grasses, legumes, and herbs — typically six or more species in total. Common grass components include perennial ryegrass, timothy, cocksfoot, and festuloliums. Common legumes include white clover, red clover, alsike clover, birdsfoot trefoil, lucerne, and sainfoin. Common herbs include ribwort plantain (Plantago lanceolata), chicory (Cichorium intybus), burnet (Sanguisorba minor), yarrow (Achillea millefolium), and sheeps parsley. The defining agronomic principle is functional diversity: deep-rooting species access subsoil moisture and minerals unavailable to shallow-rooted ryegrass; legumes fix atmospheric nitrogen reducing synthetic N inputs; herbs provide bioactive compounds with anthelmintic properties. TOMS research (Duchy College/Rothamsted, 2022) found the most prevalent species in commercial UK MSS trials were chicory, plantain, ryegrass and white clover — each contributing 15%+ to fresh weight. Ryegrass declines through the season while plantain, chicory and white clover increase, providing complementary seasonal coverage. The ley typically persists for 3–5 years before re-establishment is warranted, with the herb component often declining earlier than the grass-legume fraction.
ecology.ecological_value_notes: Higher ecological value than c1b due to floral diversity, pollinator resource (chicory, clovers, sainfoin, birdsfoot trefoil all significant nectar sources), and structural diversity. Deep-rooting species improve soil biology. Not eligible on SSSI or species-rich grassland — this is a production system, not a conservation habitat. Ecological value score 2 reflects genuine biodiversity benefit above c1b but is still primarily a managed production system.
condition_indicators_good: All sown species present and establishing — no single species dominating to exclusion of others
Deep-rooting herbs (chicory, plantain) >15% cover each and actively growing
Legume component >30% DM contribution — white clover, red clover, or other legumes visible and productive
Flowering herbs present in season — supporting pollinators
No single grass species exceeding 60% of sward by cover
Sward structurally diverse — range of heights, leaf shapes, and root architectures visible
No significant weed ingress — docks, thistles, and annual weeds <5% cover
condition_indicators_poor: Herb species (chicory, plantain, sainfoin) declining or absent — sward reverting to grass-dominant
Ryegrass or other aggressive grass dominating at >70% cover — species diversity lost
Legume component <15% — nitrogen fixation benefit lost, protein contribution falling
Rank, stemmy growth indicating under-grazing or poor rotation management
Weed ingress >15% — particularly docks (Rumex spp.) or thistles
Open patches from herb die-back — particularly chicory in wet winters or overgrazing periods
Sward >4 years from establishment without species assessment — high probability of herb decline
ecosystem_functions: Forage production — comparable to PRG/WC ley in DM yield under good management at significantly reduced N inputs; potential yield advantage in summer drought conditions due to deep-rooting species

Nitrogen fixation — legume component can fix 150–200 kg N/ha/yr; TOMS found MSS matched PRG yield at zero N vs PRG control at 250 kg N/ha in year 2 — the economic case rests here, not on yield superiority
Mineral cycling — deep-rooting species access subsoil minerals; evidence on mineral uplift is mixed (see nutritional quality notes)
Anthelmintic properties — sainfoin, birdsfoot trefoil, and chicory contain condensed tannins with documented antiparasitic effects; two TOMS farms reported reduced anthelmintic treatments
Pollinator habitat — diverse flowering species provide nectar resource significantly greater than ryegrass monocultures
Soil structure — diverse root architectures at multiple depths improve porosity, drainage, and moisture retention
Drought resilience — deep-rooting species maintain growth during dry periods when ryegrass swards fail
Bloat reduction — condensed tannins in sainfoin and birdsfoot trefoil reduce bloat risk compared to pure clover swards

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS) — field research findings
sources.author_org: Duchy College Rural Business School / Rothamsted Research North Wyke
sources.specific_claim: Biomass yields similar between MSS (zero N) and PRG control (250 kg N/ha) in year 2. Forage mineral analysis showed relatively similar trace elements between MSS and PRG/WC swards. Most prevalent species: chicory, plantain, ryegrass, white clover each >15% fresh weight. Ryegrass declines through season; plantain, chicory, WC increase. Average commercial MSS mix: 61% grass, 12% herb, 27% legume.
sources.notes: L2 — collaborative project Duchy College / Rothamsted North Wyke. Cornwall farm network plus replicated Rothamsted plots. Most robust UK-specific MSS dataset available.

sources.title: Managing herbal leys
sources.specific_claim: Herbal leys differ from grass swards by having lower sugar, high protein, and different mineral profile; DM estimation 9–15% DM; plate meters require calibration; cut-and-weigh more accurate; wet chemistry recommended over NIR for forage analysis

sources.title: CSAM3: Herbal leys — SFI action guidance
sources.specific_claim: Minimum species: 1 grass, 2 legumes, 2 herbs; typical ley lasts ~4 years before re-establishment; not eligible on SSSI or species-rich grassland; legume and herb seeds ideally >25% by weight of seed mix

sources.title: Herbal leys increase forage macro- and micronutrient content, spring lamb nutrition, liveweight gain, and reduce gastrointestinal parasites compared to a grass-clover ley
sources.specific_claim: No significant difference in CP, ME, or digestibility between herbal and grass-clover ley. Herbal ley significantly higher in macro- and micronutrients including copper in both seasons. Spring lamb liveweight gain significantly greater on herbal ley. FEC reduced 78% in spring.

sources.title: Herbal leys — overview and benefits
sources.specific_claim: Herbal leys containing >30% legumes eliminate need for synthetic N; sainfoin, birdsfoot trefoil and chicory are proven anthelmintics; red clover ~20% CP

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Prime / All species establishing
condition_grade_descriptors.description: All sown functional groups present and productive. Deep-rooting herbs (chicory, plantain, sainfoin) >15% cover each. Legumes >30% DM contribution. No single grass >60% cover. Sward structurally diverse. Typically years 1–3 post-establishment. Full ecological and production potential being realised.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Good / Some herb decline beginning
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Most species still present. Some winter losses in chicory or other susceptible herbs. Deep-rooters present but cover declining from peak. Legumes still functional at >20% DM contribution. Grasses beginning to increase as competitive pressure builds. Years 2–4. Management can slow further decline.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Moderate / Herb fraction declining significantly
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Notable herb species loss. Deep-rooting species (chicory, sainfoin) declining or patchy. Sward becoming grass-legume dominant — approaching c1b composition. Legumes still present but may be falling below 20%. Production maintained but functional diversity benefits diminishing. Reseed horizon approaching.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Poor / Herb fraction largely lost
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Deep-rooting herbs largely absent. Sward composition approaching g4 or c1b — grass dominant with some legume remnants. Functional diversity benefits (anthelmintic, drought resilience) largely lost. Mineral uplift lost. Production delivering little over a standard ryegrass ley. Reseed warranted.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Transitional / Reclassify
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Herb species absent. No functional diversity remaining. Reclassify as c1b (if ryegrass/clover dominant) or g4 (if modified permanent grassland composition). Re-establishment essential.

grade_field_indicators: Herb cover (%) — assess chicory, plantain, sainfoin, burnet individually; note presence/absence and vigour
Legume cover (%) — white clover stolon presence and vigour, red clover crown condition, birdsfoot trefoil flowering
Grass dominance — estimate % cover of perennial ryegrass and other grasses; >60% is a Grade 3–4 signal
Structural diversity — can you see three distinct plant types (grass, legume, herb) clearly represented?
Weed assessment — docks (Rumex spp.) particularly problematic in herbal leys — note % cover and size
Root architecture check — soil probe or spade: are deep root channels visible below 20cm?
Sward age — record establishment date; flag for assessment at year 3; reseed decision at year 4

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.author_org: Duchy College Rural Business School / Rothamsted Research North Wyke
sources.specific_claim: Species dynamics in commercial MSS: ryegrass declines through season; plantain, chicory and white clover increase. Most prevalent species each >15% fresh weight. Herb fraction persistency is the primary management challenge over the ley lifespan.

sources.title: CSAM3: Herbal leys — SFI action guidance
sources.specific_claim: Ley typically lasts ~4 years before re-establishment; chicory highly productive, better suited to grazing than cutting

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: CONTESTED — flag explicitly in client-facing outputs. The honest yield story from the best available evidence (TOMS, L2): MSS matched PRG yield at zero N vs PRG at 250 kg N/ha in year 2. The economic case is nitrogen saving, not yield superiority. Seed company trials (Germinal Horizon 4-year): MSS outyielded PRG-only by 26%+ — but comparison is vs PRG monoculture with high N, not PRG/WC ley. AHDB: PRG ley likely outstrips herb ley in raw t/ha. Meta-analysis (ScienceDirect 2022, L1): DM increased 1.63 t/ha per metre of additional root depth; legume-dominant swards 2.20 t/ha more than no-legume swards. On balance: Grade 1 herbal ley is broadly comparable to Grade 1 c1b in DM yield; may exceed it in drought years. Range reflects genuine uncertainty.
forage_yield_by_grade.yield_context_note: Do not present herbal ley yield advantage as established fact without citing source level and contested status. The defensible client claim is: comparable yield to PRG/WC ley at significantly lower N input.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Estimated from herb decline trajectory. As herbs decline sward approaches c1b composition and yield.
Estimated. Herb fraction largely functional but declining. Approaching c1b Grade 2 yield. Deep-rooting drought resilience benefit still partially present.
Estimated. Herb fraction largely lost. Sward delivering c1b Grade 3 equivalent yield without diversity benefits. Reseed economics compelling.

forage_yield_literature.contest_note: Yield comparison between herbal leys and PRG/WC leys is actively debated. The primary finding from the most robust UK-specific source (TOMS, Duchy College/Rothamsted, L2) is that MSS can match PRG yield with zero synthetic N vs PRG at 250 kg N/ha — the economic and environmental case rests on this, not on raw yield superiority. Do not present yield advantage claims as established fact.

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.author_org: Duchy College Rural Business School / Rothamsted Research North Wyke
sources.specific_claim: Biomass yields similar between MSS (zero N) and PRG control (250 kg N/ha) in year 2 of establishment. Most robust UK-specific MSS yield dataset.

sources.title: How multispecies swards can improve production efficiency
sources.author_org: University of Reading / Duchy College / Rothamsted Research / Cotswold Seeds (BBSRC SARIC funded)
sources.specific_claim: 5-year study: biomass yields similar across control (PRG+250kgN) and MSS plots in year 2. Steers finished ~400kg, DLWG 1.1 kg on MSS. Reduction in methane emissions where MSS fed. Performance varies widely across locations.
sources.notes: L1 via BBSRC-funded 5-year collaborative study. Farmers Weekly report of findings from SCI conference presentation. Primary paper not yet retrieved — flag for follow-up.

sources.title: Rotational grazing and multispecies herbal leys increase productivity in temperate pastoral systems — A meta-analysis
sources.specific_claim: DM increased 1.63 t/ha per metre of increased sward root depth; legume-dominant swards yielded 2.20 t/ha more than no-legume swards

sources.title: Using Multi-Species Swards for Sustainable Livestock Production
sources.specific_claim: 4-year Wiltshire trial: 6-way MSS outyielded PRG-only by 26%+ average annual yield. Comparison vs PRG monoculture with 250 kg N/ha — not vs PRG/WC ley.
sources.notes: L4 — seed company trial. Interpret with caution. Comparison group is PRG monoculture, not standard PRG/WC ley.

sources.title: Where do herbal leys sit in a modern dairy system?
sources.specific_claim: PRG ley likely outstrips herb ley in raw t/ha; margin per hectare is the more relevant metric. ME comparable; CP slightly higher later in season; minerals higher in herbal leys in this trial.

nutritional_quality_by_grade.me_notes: ME broadly comparable to PRG/WC ley. Average ME density 11.5 MJ/kg DM (AHDB/First Milk). AHDB White Peak trial: ME comparable to ryegrass. Cooledge et al 2024 (L1): no significant difference in ME between herbal and grass-clover ley. Herbs (10.5–11.5 MJ/kg DM in spring, can fall below 10 MJ if mature or flowering) slightly lower than peak PRG; legumes compensate.
nutritional_quality_by_grade.cp_notes: CP is highly variable and mix-dependent. AHDB White Peak trial: CP slightly higher than ryegrass, particularly later in season. Cooledge et al 2024 (L1): no significant difference in overall CP between herbal and grass-clover ley. ABP Bromstead farm trial: first-cut herbal silage CP 8.5% vs 12.5% ryegrass-clover; second-cut 14.6% vs 15.8% — silage CP particularly variable. Legume content controls protein level: leys with >30% legumes will have higher CP.
nutritional_quality_by_grade.mineral_notes: CONTESTED — do not overstate. Evidence is genuinely mixed between studies. Cooledge et al 2024 (L1): significantly higher macro- and micronutrients in herbal ley vs grass-clover, including copper, in both seasons. TOMS 2022 (L2): forage mineral analysis showed relatively similar trace elements between MSS and PRG/WC swards. AHDB White Peak trial: minerals higher in herbal ley. The honest position: mineral uplift is plausible and supported by some trials but not consistent across the evidence base. Deep-rooting mineral access is a sound mechanistic argument but the field evidence is mixed. Flag as 'evidence emerging, not settled' in client-facing outputs.

sources.title: Herbal leys increase forage macro- and micronutrient content, spring lamb nutrition, liveweight gain, and reduce gastrointestinal parasites compared to a grass-clover ley
sources.specific_claim: No significant difference in CP, ME, digestibility between herbal and grass-clover ley. Significantly higher macro- and micronutrients including copper in herbal ley in both seasons. Spring lamb FEC reduced 78%. DLWG significantly greater on herbal ley in spring.

sources.title: Toolbox of Multi-species Swards (TOMS)
sources.author_org: Duchy College Rural Business School / Rothamsted Research North Wyke
sources.specific_claim: Autumn 2022 forage analysis: relatively similar trace elements between MSS and PRG/WC swards. Two farms reported reduced anthelmintic treatments for MSS-grazed animals.
sources.notes: Contradicts Cooledge 2024 on mineral uplift — both are credible sources. Difference may reflect species composition, soil type, or season. Do not adjudicate — store both, flag as contested.

sources.title: Where do herbal leys sit in a modern dairy system?
sources.specific_claim: ME comparable to ryegrass; CP slightly higher particularly later in season; minerals higher — supports deep-rooting mineral mining theory

sources.title: Herbal v ryegrass-clover leys: Cattle performance on test
sources.specific_claim: First-cut herbal silage CP 8.5% vs 12.5% ryegrass-clover; second-cut 14.6% vs 15.8%. Animal performance broadly comparable over 269-day trial.
sources.notes: L5 — single farm trial. Important practical data on silage CP variability by cut.

nutritional_quality_by_grade.mineral_notes: Mineral profile beginning to decline as herb fraction reduces. Deep-rooting species still present but at lower density.
Mineral uplift largely lost as herb fraction declines. Approaching grass-legume mineral profile.
Mineral profile now approaching c1b or g4 equivalent. Functional benefits of herbal ley largely lost.

optimal_grazing_animals.breed: Beef cattle (all native and commercial breeds)
optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Well suited. Rotational grazing at 28–38 day rest periods maintains herb fraction. BBSRC SARIC 5-year study: steers finished ~400kg, DLWG 1.1 kg on MSS. ABP Bromstead: performance equivalent to PRG/WC ley over full finishing period.

Excellent — particularly for parasite management. Cooledge et al 2024: spring lamb FEC reduced 78% on herbal ley vs grass-clover ley. Chicory, sainfoin, and birdsfoot trefoil anthelmintic effect documented. Rotational grazing essential to maintain palatability.

Suitable. Innovative Farmers trial: no significant difference in milk yield between PRG/WC and MSS groups. ME comparable. Mineral uplift may benefit fertility and health — evidence emerging but not settled.
Suitable on well-drained ground. Diverse root system more resilient to rooting damage than ryegrass monoculture.

unsuitable_animals.notes: Set-stocking is incompatible with herbal ley management. Selective grazing rapidly eliminates palatable herb species. Rotational rest is essential to maintain sward diversity.

timing_windows.grazing_rotation_notes: Longer rotation than PRG ley essential. Chicory becomes woody and unpalatable at >38 days without grazing. Enter at sward height appropriate to species mix — not calibrated to plate meter; use cut-and-weigh or visual assessment.
timing_windows.rest_periods: Rest periods of 5+ consecutive weeks between May and late July recommended under SFI CSAM3 — allows flowering and pollinator benefit.
timing_windows.avoid: Set-stocking. Overgrazing in wet conditions — poaching destroys tap-rooted herbs. Grazing chicory below 5cm in winter — crown damage kills plants. Heavy grazing pressure in autumn.

management.intensity_guidance: Moderate stocking intensity. The key management objective is maintaining species diversity, not maximising utilisation. Rotational grazing at 28–38 day intervals prevents herb species being selectively grazed out. Legumes: target >30% DM contribution. Docks: greatest weed management challenge — very limited herbicide options; prevention via clean seedbed is preferable to cure. Re-establishment: plan for year 4–5.
management.measurement_note: IMPORTANT: Plate meters and compressed sward sticks are NOT calibrated for herbal leys and will give inaccurate DM estimates. Use cut-and-weigh for accurate DM measurement. NIR analysis not reliable for herbal ley forage quality — use wet chemistry (NDF, CP, fibre fractions). Source: AHDB managing herbal leys.
management.monitoring_protocol: Monthly during growing season: visual species assessment — are all three functional groups (grass, legume, herb) visible and productive? Annual: photograph fixed points; estimate % cover of key herb species. Flag: chicory or sainfoin cover falling below 10% as Grade 2 signal. Year 3: formal species assessment to trigger reseed decision. Dock monitoring: seedling emergence in year 1–2 is critical management point.

fields_partially_populated: forage_yield_by_grade — Grade 1 medium confidence with contested flag; Grades 2–4 estimated
nutritional_quality_by_grade — Grade 1 medium confidence; mineral data contested between studies; Grades 2–4 estimated
fields_pending_research: Specific quantified mineral data (Cu, Co, Se, Zn, Ca, P, Mg) per grade from L1-L2 sources — referenced qualitatively, not yet quantified
Primary BBSRC SARIC 5-year study paper — findings cited via Farmers Weekly report; primary publication not yet retrieved
Scottish and upland-specific herbal ley yield data — most trial data is lowland England and Cornwall
Multi-year SRUC Barony data — 2023 trial is one season only; follow-up data needed
data_completeness.honest_assessment: c1b7 is the most data-contested record in the toolbox. The evidence base is growing rapidly (most peer-reviewed data 2020–2024) but remains genuinely mixed. Three findings are robust and defensible: (1) ME is comparable to PRG/WC ley across multiple studies; (2) MSS can match PRG yield at zero N vs PRG at 250 kg N/ha — the economic and environmental case rests here; (3) antiparasitic effects are documented at L1 (Cooledge 2024) and supported by farmer observations in TOMS. Mineral uplift claims are contested — higher in some trials (Cooledge 2024, AHDB White Peak), similar in others (TOMS 2022) — stored honestly as contested, not adjudicated. The client-facing case for c1b7 rests on nitrogen saving, parasite burden reduction, drought resilience, and pollinator value. Do not present yield superiority or mineral uplift as established fact without citing source level and contested status.
